title: Topic 61: The Endowment Effect in Gambling: Why We Overvalue Our Bets
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper explores the cognitive quirk known as the endowment effect, a phenomenon that causes individuals to irrationally overvalue items they own, and its peculiar manifestation in the world of gambling. We delve into the psychological underpinnings of why a betting slip clutched in a sweaty palm suddenly feels more valuable than the cash it cost, and why a fantasy football team assembled by the owner is clearly superior to all others, despite all evidence to the contrary. Through a synthesis of behavioral economics, psychological studies, and real-world gambling anecdotes, this paper argues that the endowment effect is a powerful, often hilarious, force in the casino, at the racetrack, and in the digital realm of online betting. We will demonstrate that understanding this bias is not only key to understanding the modern gambler but also provides a window into the often-illogical landscape of human decision-making. In short, we’re all suckers for our own stuff, and this paper explains why, with academic rigor and a healthy dose of schadenfreude.

### INTRODUCTION

Have you ever noticed that the moment you buy a lottery ticket, it transforms? It’s no longer a flimsy piece of paper with a one-in-a-billion chance of making you rich. No, it becomes a vessel of dreams, a tangible manifestation of your future yacht. You wouldn’t trade it for another ticket, even one with the exact same odds, because *this one* is yours. This, my friends, is the endowment effect in action, a psychological gremlin that whispers sweet nothings in our ears, telling us that our possessions are inherently more valuable simply because we own them. It’s the reason we hold onto that ugly sweater from Aunt Mildred, the one that makes us look like a deranged sheep, and the reason we refuse to sell our fantasy football players, even when they’re performing with all the athletic prowess of a sloth on tranquilizers.

In the high-stakes world of gambling, this cognitive bias isn’t just a harmless quirk; it’s a major player at the table. The endowment effect in gambling is the invisible hand that guides our bets, inflates our confidence, and often, empties our wallets. It’s the voice that tells a poker player that their pair of deuces is a sleeping giant, a future full house in the making, simply because those deuces are *their* deuces. It’s the force that convinces a sports bettor that their wager on the underdog is a stroke of genius, a contrarian masterpiece, even when the underdog is a team of asthmatic pugs playing against a pack of cheetahs. This paper will take a deep, and hopefully humorous, dive into the endowment effect’s role in the gambling world. We will explore why we overvalue our bets, how this bias is exploited by the gambling industry, and what, if anything, we can do to resist its siren song. We will journey from the hallowed halls of academia, where behavioral economists first identified this peculiar phenomenon, to the sticky floors of the casino, where it plays out in real-time, with real money on the line. So, place your bets, hold onto your hats, and prepare to have your irrational decision-making processes laid bare. It’s going to be an enlightening, and perhaps slightly uncomfortable, ride.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has a long and storied history of studying the peculiar ways in which the human brain malfunctions. The endowment effect is one of its greatest hits, a chart-topping single in the behavioral economics jukebox. The seminal work in this field, the academic equivalent of the Beatles’ *Sgt. Pepper’s Lonely Hearts Club Band*, comes from the holy trinity of Daniel Kahneman, Jack Knetsch, and Richard Thaler. In their now-legendary 1990 study, they gifted unsuspecting university students with coffee mugs, a seemingly innocuous act that would ripple through the decades. When they later tried to buy the mugs back, they discovered that the students, now proud mug owners, demanded twice the price they themselves would have been willing to pay for the same mug just minutes earlier. This discrepancy between “Willingness to Accept” (WTA) and “Willingness to Pay” (WTP) is the statistical signature of the endowment effect, the mathematical proof that our brains are, to put it mildly, a bit weird.

Kahneman and his colleagues attributed this phenomenon to “loss aversion,” the idea that losing something hurts about twice as much as gaining something of equal value feels good. In other words, the pain of giving up your precious, university-branded mug is far greater than the joy of acquiring a new one. This is why we cling to our possessions like a drowning man to a life raft, even when those possessions are, objectively speaking, junk. The literature is filled with further evidence of this bias. Carmon and Ariely (2000) found that the endowment effect is even more pronounced with more desirable items, discovering that the asking price for NCAA Final Four tickets was a whopping 14 times higher than the offer price. It seems the more we want something, the more our ownership of it inflates its value in our minds, turning a simple ticket into a golden ticket to Willy Wonka’s chocolate factory.

While the endowment effect has been studied in a variety of contexts, from real estate to fine art, its application to the world of gambling is a particularly fertile ground for research. The work of scholars like Jonathan Bales has brought the endowment effect into the realm of fantasy sports, where it runs rampant. Bales notes that fantasy sports players are notoriously reluctant to trade their players, overvaluing their own team to a comical degree. This is the fantasy football equivalent of a mother who thinks her child is the most beautiful baby in the world, even if the baby looks like a potato with a face drawn on it. The research suggests that the simple act of “drafting” a player, of claiming them as your own, is enough to trigger the endowment effect, turning a mediocre running back into a future Hall of Famer in the owner’s mind. This body of literature, while often dry and academic, paints a clear and consistent picture: we are all, to some extent, victims of our own ownership, and in the world of gambling, this can be a very expensive habit indeed.

### ANALYSIS

So, what’s really going on in our brains when we refuse to trade a lottery ticket or cling to a losing stock? The endowment effect, at its core, is a cocktail of cognitive biases, a potent brew of irrationality that makes us all a little bit crazy. The main ingredient in this cocktail is, as we’ve discussed, **loss aversion**. The thought of losing something we own, even something as trivial as a coffee mug, triggers a primal fear in our brains. It’s the same fear that made our ancestors cling to their last piece of mammoth meat, and it’s the same fear that makes us cling to a losing poker hand. The pain of folding, of admitting defeat, is often greater than the rational calculation of the odds. We’ve invested in this hand, we’ve nurtured it, we’ve watched it grow from two measly cards into a beautiful, albeit statistically improbable, work of art. To give it up now would be a betrayal, a personal loss that transcends the monetary value of the pot.

But loss aversion is only part of the story. The endowment effect is also fueled by a powerful sense of **psychological ownership**. The moment we acquire something, it becomes an extension of ourselves. Our bets are not just bets; they are a reflection of our intelligence, our intuition, our very being. A winning bet is not just a financial gain; it’s a validation of our superior intellect. A losing bet, on the other hand, is a personal failure, an indictment of our judgment. This is why we’re so reluctant to admit when we’ve made a bad bet. To do so would be to admit that we are not the brilliant, all-knowing prognosticators we imagine ourselves to be. It’s much easier to double down, to convince ourselves that our losing horse is just having a bad day, that our losing stock is just a sleeping giant, waiting for the right moment to awaken.

The gambling industry, of course, is well aware of these psychological shenanigans. They are masters of exploiting our cognitive biases, and the endowment effect is one of their favorite toys. The very language of gambling is designed to foster a sense of ownership. We don’t just place bets; we “buy in,” we “take a position,” we “have a stake in the game.” The chips, the tickets, the virtual currency – they are all designed to feel like tangible possessions, to trigger that warm, fuzzy feeling of ownership. The more we feel like we own our bets, the more we’re likely to overvalue them, and the more we’re likely to keep playing, even when the odds are stacked against us. The casino, in essence, is a giant laboratory for the study of the endowment effect, and we, the gamblers, are the unwitting test subjects. We are the rats in the maze, and the cheese is the fleeting, illusory feeling of ownership.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The endowment effect in gambling isn’t just a theoretical concept; it’s a real-world phenomenon with real-world consequences. Take, for example, the case of the “lucky” lottery ticket. We’ve all heard stories of people who refuse to sell their lottery tickets, even for a guaranteed profit. In 2016, a man in California won a $1 million lottery prize. Before the prize was officially announced, a local news station offered him $50,000 for the ticket, a significant sum of money for a piece of paper that could, in theory, be worthless. The man refused, and as luck would have it, he won the million. But what if he hadn’t? What if the ticket had been a dud? The endowment effect would have cost him a cool $50,000. This is the power of ownership. The moment he bought that ticket, it became more than just a ticket; it became a symbol of hope, a dream of a better life. To sell it would have been to sell that dream, and that, for many people, is a price too high to pay.

Another classic example of the endowment effect in action can be found in the world of horse racing. Go to any racetrack, and you’ll see them: the old-timers, the grizzled veterans who have been betting on the same horse, or the same jockey, for years. They have their “lucky” numbers, their “lucky” horses, their “lucky” hats. They have, in essence, endowed these things with a value that far exceeds their objective worth. A horse is no longer just a horse; it’s “their” horse. A jockey is no longer just a jockey; he’s “their” jockey. This sense of ownership can lead to some truly irrational betting patterns. A gambler might continue to bet on a losing horse, not because they have any rational reason to believe it will win, but because they have a personal connection to it. They’ve invested in it, they’ve cheered for it, they’ve shared in its defeats. To abandon it now would be a betrayal, a violation of the sacred bond between a man and his horse.

And then, of course, there’s the world of online sports betting, where the endowment effect has found a new and fertile breeding ground. The rise of in-play betting, where you can bet on a game as it’s happening, has taken the endowment effect to a whole new level. A bettor might place a pre-game wager on a team, and then, as the game unfolds, they become more and more invested in that team. They’re not just a neutral observer anymore; they’re a fan, a supporter, a part of the team. This can lead to a dangerous form of “in-play blindness,” where the bettor is so emotionally invested in their initial bet that they fail to see the writing on the wall. They might ignore the fact that their team is playing like a bunch of hungover amateurs, that their star player has just been carted off the field with a broken leg. They cling to their initial bet, their endowed position, like a captain going down with his ship. In the world of gambling, as in life, it seems that letting go is the hardest part.

### DISCUSSION

The endowment effect, as we have seen, is a powerful and pervasive force in the world of gambling. But what are the broader implications of this cognitive bias? What does it tell us about human behavior, and how can we use this knowledge to make better decisions, both at the casino and in our everyday lives? The endowment effect, in many ways, is a window into the irrational, emotional, and often-hilarious world of human decision-making. It reminds us that we are not the rational, calculating machines that traditional economics would have us believe. We are, instead, a messy, contradictory, and deeply human mix of logic and emotion, of reason and instinct.

Understanding the endowment effect can help us to become more savvy consumers. The next time you’re offered a “free trial” or a “money-back guarantee,” remember the endowment effect. The company is not just being generous; they’re trying to get you to take ownership of their product, to endow it with a value that will make it harder for you to part with. The next time you’re haggling over the price of a used car, remember the endowment effect. The seller is not just being greedy; they have a personal connection to that car, a history of memories and experiences that are baked into its value. By understanding the psychological forces at play, we can approach these situations with a more critical and discerning eye.

And what about the world of gambling? Can we, as gamblers, overcome the endowment effect? The answer, I believe, is a qualified “yes.” We can’t eliminate the bias entirely; it’s too deeply ingrained in our cognitive architecture. But we can, with a little bit of self-awareness and a lot of discipline, learn to recognize it and mitigate its effects. We can learn to treat our bets as what they are: a means to an end, a tool for making money, not a reflection of our self-worth. We can learn to be more objective, to evaluate our bets based on the evidence, not on our emotional attachment to them. We can learn, in short, to be more like a professional gambler and less like a love-struck teenager. It’s not easy, but it’s not impossible. And in the world of gambling, where the margins are thin and the competition is fierce, it can make all the difference.

### CONCLUSION

In the grand casino of life, we are all gamblers, and the endowment effect is the house edge that we bring to the table ourselves. It’s the cognitive bias that makes us cling to our losing stocks, our failing businesses, and our terrible fantasy football teams. It’s the voice in our head that tells us that our stuff is special, that our bets are smarter, that our lottery tickets are destined for greatness. As we have seen, this bias is not just a harmless quirk; it’s a powerful and often-costly force that shapes our decisions and our destinies. But by shining a light on this irrational corner of our minds, by understanding the psychological forces that pull our strings, we can begin to cut ourselves free. We can learn to let go of our losing hands, to trade our mediocre players, and to see our possessions for what they are: not as extensions of ourselves, but as tools to be used and, when necessary, to be discarded. The endowment effect may be a part of our human nature, but it doesn’t have to be our destiny. And that, my friends, is a bet worth making.
references:
Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1990). Experimental tests of the endowment effect and the Coase theorem. Journal of Political Economy, 98(6), 1325-1348.
Carmon, Z., & Ariely, D. (2000). Focusing on the forgone: How value can appear so different to buyers and sellers. Journal of Consumer Research, 27(3), 360-370.
Bales, J. (2021, September 23). Bales: How to Overcome & Exploit Endowment Effect and Gambler’s Fallacy in Betting & Fantasy Sports. The Action Network. Retrieved from https://www.actionnetwork.com/education/jonathan-bales-endowment-effect-gamblers-fallacy-betting-fantasy-sports
